question_text: The New Economic Policy of 1991 introduced three major reforms known as:
explanation: The New Economic Policy (NEP) of 1991, introduced by the Government of India under PM Narasimha Rao and Finance Minister Dr. Manmohan Singh, had three pillars: Liberalisation, Privatisation, and Globalisation (LPG). These reforms were introduced to address the severe balance of payments crisis and open up the Indian economy.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Licensing, Planning, and Government control
Liberalisation, Privatisation, and Globalisation
Localisation, Protectionism, and Growth
Liquidation, Privatisation, and Governance

elimination_hints.hint: Licensing, planning, and government control were features of the pre-1991 economy. The 1991 reforms moved AWAY from these.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing pre-reform features with the reforms themselves

elimination_hints.hint: Localisation and Protectionism are the OPPOSITE of the 1991 reforms. The reforms promoted globalisation and reduced protectionism.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the direction of the reforms

elimination_hints.hint: Liquidation means closing down businesses. The reforms aimed to revive the economy, not liquidate it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing liberalisation with liquidation

question_text: Liberalisation means:
explanation: Liberalisation means removing unnecessary restrictions, controls, and regulations on businesses. Before 1991, businesses needed licences for setting up factories, expanding capacity, importing goods, etc. (called 'Licence Raj'). Liberalisation abolished or reduced these controls to allow businesses to operate more freely and competitively.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Removing unnecessary government restrictions and controls on businesses
Transferring government-owned businesses to the private sector
Integrating the Indian economy with the world economy
Increasing government control over private businesses

elimination_hints.hint: Transferring government businesses to the private sector is Privatisation, not Liberalisation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing liberalisation with privatisation

elimination_hints.hint: Integrating with the world economy is Globalisation, not Liberalisation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing liberalisation with globalisation

elimination_hints.hint: Increasing government control is the OPPOSITE of liberalisation. Liberalisation means REDUCING controls.
elimination_hints.misconception: Completely reversing the meaning of liberalisation

question_text: Privatisation refers to:
explanation: Privatisation means transferring ownership and management of public sector enterprises (government-owned businesses) to the private sector, either fully or partially. This includes selling government equity (disinvestment), allowing private sector participation in sectors previously reserved for government, and reducing the role of public sector in economic activity.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Making all private information of businesses public
Removing restrictions on imports and exports
Transferring ownership of government enterprises to the private sector
Giving private loans to government companies

elimination_hints.hint: 'Private' in privatisation refers to private SECTOR (businesses), not private information. It is about ownership transfer, not disclosure.
elimination_hints.misconception: Literal misinterpretation of 'privatisation'

elimination_hints.hint: Removing import/export restrictions is part of Liberalisation and trade reforms, not Privatisation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing privatisation with trade liberalisation

elimination_hints.hint: Privatisation is about ownership transfer, not lending. Giving loans is a banking/financial function.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing ownership transfer with financial assistance

question_text: Globalisation means:
explanation: Globalisation means integrating the Indian economy with the world economy. It involves free flow of goods, services, capital, technology, and labour across national borders. Globalisation is achieved through reducing trade barriers, encouraging foreign investment, and promoting international cooperation. It makes the world a single market.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Expanding a business to all cities within India
Manufacturing goods using global raw materials only
Making all government services available online globally
Integrating the domestic economy with the world economy through free flow of trade and investment

elimination_hints.hint: Expanding within India is domestic expansion, not globalisation. Globalisation involves crossing national boundaries.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing domestic expansion with international integration

elimination_hints.hint: Globalisation is not just about raw materials. It covers goods, services, capital, technology, and labour flows across borders.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reducing globalisation to just raw material sourcing

elimination_hints.hint: E-governance is about digital government services. Globalisation is an economic concept about international trade and investment integration.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing e-governance with globalisation

question_text: The sale of government equity in public sector enterprises to private investors is known as __________.
explanation: Disinvestment (also spelled divestment) is the process of selling government equity (shares/ownership stake) in public sector enterprises to private investors. It is a key strategy under privatisation. For example, when the government sells its shares in Air India to Tata Group, it is disinvestment. The purpose is to raise revenue, improve efficiency, and reduce the government's role in business.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Nationalisation
Disinvestment
Liberalisation
Devaluation

elimination_hints.hint: Nationalisation is the OPPOSITE — it means the government TAKING OVER private businesses. Disinvestment means the government SELLING its businesses.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing nationalisation with privatisation/disinvestment

elimination_hints.hint: Liberalisation means removing restrictions on business. Selling government equity is specifically called disinvestment, a tool of privatisation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the broader reform with the specific tool

elimination_hints.hint: Devaluation means reducing the value of the domestic currency. It is a monetary policy tool, not related to selling government shares.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing devaluation (currency) with disinvestment (equity)

question_text: Before 1991, an Indian businessman needed a government licence to set up a new factory, another licence to expand capacity, import licences for foreign machinery, and approval for foreign collaboration. After the 1991 reforms, most of these requirements were abolished. Which component of the 1991 reforms is being described?
explanation: This describes Liberalisation. The pre-1991 system of extensive licensing and government approvals was called the 'Licence Raj'. Liberalisation abolished most industrial licences, reduced import restrictions, and simplified foreign collaboration approvals. The aim was to free businesses from bureaucratic controls and promote competition and efficiency.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Liberalisation
Privatisation
Globalisation
Demonetisation

elimination_hints.hint: Privatisation is about transferring government-owned companies to the private sector. The scenario describes removing regulatory controls, which is Liberalisation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing deregulation with ownership transfer

elimination_hints.hint: Globalisation is about integrating with the world economy. The scenario describes reducing domestic controls and licences — that is internal liberalisation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing domestic deregulation with international integration

elimination_hints.hint: Demonetisation is about cancelling legal tender status of currency notes. This scenario is about industrial licensing reform.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing demonetisation with economic reforms

question_text: Which of the following was NOT a feature of liberalisation in India after 1991?
explanation: Liberalisation measures included: abolition of industrial licensing (except for a few sensitive industries), freedom to import technology and capital goods, removal of restrictions on foreign investment, and deregulation of the financial sector. However, nationalisation of private banks was a pre-1991 policy (1969 and 1980) — it is the opposite of liberalisation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Abolition of industrial licensing for most industries
Freedom to import foreign technology
Increase in FDI (Foreign Direct Investment) limits
Nationalisation of private banks

elimination_hints.hint: Abolishing industrial licensing was a key liberalisation measure. It freed businesses from the need for government permission to set up or expand.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking licensing abolition is not part of liberalisation

elimination_hints.hint: Freedom to import technology was a major liberalisation reform, allowing Indian businesses access to global technology.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking technology import freedom is not liberalisation

elimination_hints.hint: Increasing FDI limits was a key reform — it allowed more foreign investment in Indian companies, opening up the economy.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking FDI liberalisation is not part of the reforms

question_text: The impact of LPG reforms on Indian businesses included:
explanation: LPG reforms had several impacts: (1) Increased competition from foreign companies entering India, (2) More choices for consumers, (3) Market-determined prices instead of government-controlled prices, (4) Rapid technological advancement, (5) Need for greater efficiency. Indian businesses had to become competitive or face decline.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Reduced competition as foreign firms were still banned
Increased competition, more consumer choices, and need for greater efficiency
Government started controlling prices of all products
Indian companies were prohibited from exporting goods

elimination_hints.hint: LPG reforms OPENED UP the market to foreign firms. Competition increased dramatically — MNCs like Coca-Cola, Samsung, and Amazon entered India.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking LPG reduced competition

elimination_hints.hint: Liberalisation moved AWAY from government price controls. Prices became market-determined through competition.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing liberalisation with increased government control

elimination_hints.hint: LPG reforms ENCOURAGED exports by removing restrictions. Indian IT companies like TCS and Infosys became major global exporters.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking reforms restricted exports

question_text: Statement: Globalisation has made it possible for Indian companies to set up operations abroad and for foreign companies to invest in India.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. Globalisation works both ways: (1) Indian companies can invest and operate abroad — Tata Motors bought Jaguar Land Rover (UK), Infosys has offices worldwide, Reliance operates globally; (2) Foreign companies can invest in India — Amazon, Walmart (Flipkart), Samsung have major Indian operations. This two-way flow of investment and trade is the essence of globalisation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: Think of Tata owning Jaguar, Infosys operating in 50+ countries, Amazon operating in India, Samsung manufacturing in India — globalisation enables two-way business flow.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking globalisation is one-directional (only foreign companies entering India)

question_text: After the 1991 reforms, Maruti Suzuki (a joint venture) faced competition from Hyundai, Honda, Toyota, and Ford entering India. The company responded by launching new models, improving quality, and reducing prices. Which aspect of the 1991 reforms DIRECTLY caused this competitive pressure?
explanation: Globalisation directly caused this competitive pressure. By integrating India with the world economy and reducing barriers to foreign investment and imports, globalisation enabled international automobile companies to enter the Indian market. Maruti had to compete on quality, variety, and price — demonstrating how globalisation increases competition and ultimately benefits consumers.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Privatisation — because Maruti was partly government-owned
Liberalisation — because industrial licensing was abolished
Globalisation — because foreign companies were allowed to enter and compete in India
Demonetisation — because it changed consumer purchasing patterns

elimination_hints.hint: Privatisation affects government ownership. The scenario is about foreign competitors entering India — that is globalisation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing ownership changes with market entry of foreign firms

elimination_hints.hint: Liberalisation removed domestic restrictions. While it facilitated reforms, the DIRECT cause of Hyundai, Honda, Toyota entering India was globalisation (opening to foreign firms).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing domestic deregulation with international market opening

elimination_hints.hint: Demonetisation happened in 2016, not 1991. The scenario describes post-1991 competition from foreign auto companies.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing demonetisation timeline with 1991 reforms

question_text: Which of the following is an example of privatisation in India?
explanation: The sale of Air India to Tata Group in 2022 is a prominent example of privatisation (specifically, strategic disinvestment). The government transferred its entire ownership stake to a private entity. Similarly, VSNL to Tata, BALCO to Sterlite, and Hindustan Zinc to Vedanta are other examples of privatisation through disinvestment.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Government setting up a new public sector steel plant
RBI reducing the repo rate to promote lending
India joining the World Trade Organisation (WTO)
Sale of Air India by the government to Tata Group

elimination_hints.hint: Setting up a NEW public sector company is the opposite of privatisation. Privatisation means TRANSFERRING government companies to private hands.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing public sector creation with privatisation

elimination_hints.hint: RBI changing repo rate is a monetary policy action, not privatisation. Privatisation involves ownership transfer of enterprises.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing monetary policy with privatisation

elimination_hints.hint: Joining WTO is a step towards globalisation (international trade integration), not privatisation (ownership transfer).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing globalisation with privatisation

question_text:
Assertion (A): After liberalisation, Indian businesses had to become more competitive and customer-focused.
Reason (R): Liberalisation removed protective barriers, exposing Indian businesses to domestic and international competition.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Before 1991, Indian businesses were protected by high tariffs, import restrictions, and licensing. They had little incentive to innovate or improve quality. When liberalisation removed these protective barriers (R), businesses suddenly faced competition from foreign companies and had to improve quality, reduce costs, and focus on customers to survive (A).
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY businesses had to become competitive — removal of barriers exposed them to competition. Clear cause-effect.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising the causal link between barrier removal and increased competition

elimination_hints.hint: R is factually correct — liberalisation DID remove protective barriers like high tariffs and licensing requirements.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly denying that liberalisation removed barriers

elimination_hints.hint: A is correct — Indian businesses genuinely had to become competitive post-1991. Companies like Ambassador cars that didn't adapt declined.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the competitive impact of liberalisation

question_text: Which of the following is NOT a measure taken under globalisation in India?
explanation: Measures under globalisation include: reduction of import duties (tariffs), removal of quantitative restrictions on imports, allowing FDI in most sectors, Indian companies investing abroad, and joining international organisations like WTO. Banning foreign companies completely would be anti-globalisation — the opposite of what globalisation promotes.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Reduction of import duties on foreign goods
Allowing FDI in retail, insurance, and defence sectors
Complete ban on foreign companies operating in India
Indian companies acquiring businesses abroad

elimination_hints.hint: Reducing import duties (tariffs) is a core globalisation measure — it makes foreign goods more affordable and increases trade.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking tariff reduction is not globalisation

elimination_hints.hint: Allowing FDI in various sectors opens India to foreign investment — a key globalisation step.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising FDI liberalisation as globalisation

elimination_hints.hint: Indian companies acquiring foreign businesses (Tata-JLR, Wipro acquisitions) is outward globalisation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking globalisation is only inward (foreign to India)

question_text: The Indian government's decision to allow 100% FDI in single-brand retail and up to 51% in multi-brand retail is an example of:
explanation: Allowing FDI in retail is an example of both Liberalisation (removing restrictions on foreign investment) and Globalisation (integrating India with global retail chains). However, since the question specifically mentions FDI policy — which directly opens India's market to foreign companies — this is most directly an example of Globalisation in the retail sector.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Privatisation
Demonetisation
Globalisation
Nationalisation

elimination_hints.hint: Privatisation involves selling government enterprises to private hands. FDI in retail is about allowing foreign companies to invest — that is globalisation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing foreign investment policy with government disinvestment

elimination_hints.hint: Demonetisation is about withdrawing currency notes from circulation. FDI policy is about foreign investment — completely different.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing unrelated economic policies

elimination_hints.hint: Nationalisation means GOVERNMENT taking over private businesses. Allowing FDI is the opposite direction — opening up to foreign private investors.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing nationalisation with foreign investment liberalisation

question_text: Hindustan Motors' iconic Ambassador car dominated India for decades with almost no competition. After 1991, when Hyundai, Maruti, Honda, and Toyota launched modern cars at competitive prices, Ambassador sales declined sharply and the company eventually shut down its car division. What does this example illustrate about the impact of LPG reforms?
explanation: This illustrates that LPG reforms exposed uncompetitive Indian businesses to intense competition. Companies that were complacent due to government protection were unable to adapt when foreign competitors entered. The Ambassador case shows that businesses must adapt to the changing environment (improve quality, innovation, customer focus) or face decline. It highlights the 'survival of the fittest' aspect of the competitive post-reform environment.
subtopic: Impact of LPG — need for adaptation
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: LPG reforms were harmful and should have been reversed
Businesses that do not adapt to the changing environment face decline or closure
Indian businesses cannot compete with foreign companies under any circumstances
The government should have banned foreign car companies from entering India

elimination_hints.hint: While some companies suffered, overall LPG reforms benefited India — better products, more jobs, higher growth. The reform isn't harmful; companies must adapt.
elimination_hints.misconception: Blaming the reforms instead of the company's failure to adapt

elimination_hints.hint: Many Indian companies thrived post-reforms — Maruti, Tata Motors, Mahindra all became globally competitive. Ambassador failed because it didn't innovate.
elimination_hints.misconception: Generalising one company's failure to all Indian businesses

elimination_hints.hint: Banning foreign companies would be protectionism (opposite of globalisation). It would deprive consumers of choice and reduce competitive pressure for quality.
elimination_hints.misconception: Advocating protectionism instead of recognising the need for adaptation

question_text: Which of the following was a reason for introducing the New Economic Policy of 1991?
explanation: India faced a severe Balance of Payments (BoP) crisis in 1991 — foreign exchange reserves had fallen so low that India could barely pay for two weeks of imports. The government had to pledge gold reserves to avoid defaulting on international payments. This crisis forced India to approach the IMF and World Bank for loans, which came with conditions to liberalise the economy.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Severe Balance of Payments crisis and depleted foreign exchange reserves
India had excess foreign exchange and wanted to invest abroad
Indian businesses were too competitive and needed protection
India wanted to reduce its economic growth rate

elimination_hints.hint: India had a SHORTAGE of foreign exchange, not excess. Reserves had fallen to barely 2 weeks of imports — a crisis, not abundance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Completely reversing the actual economic situation

elimination_hints.hint: Indian businesses were UNCOMPETITIVE due to protection. The reforms aimed to increase competition, not provide more protection.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the cause-effect relationship

elimination_hints.hint: The reforms aimed to INCREASE growth, not reduce it. The economy was stagnating and needed liberalisation to boost growth.
elimination_hints.misconception: Misunderstanding the purpose of the reforms

question_text: The concept of 'Make in India' launched in 2014 is primarily aimed at:
explanation: 'Make in India' is a government initiative to encourage domestic and foreign companies to manufacture their products in India. It aims to boost the manufacturing sector, create employment, attract FDI, and develop a robust manufacturing infrastructure. It is an extension of globalisation — inviting global companies to set up manufacturing bases in India.
topic: Impact of Government Policy Changes on Business
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Banning all imports to protect domestic industries
Nationalising all private manufacturing companies
Reducing India's population through manufacturing jobs
Encouraging manufacturing in India to boost growth, create jobs, and attract FDI

elimination_hints.hint: Make in India invites foreign companies to MANUFACTURE in India, not to ban imports. It promotes production, not protectionism.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing manufacturing promotion with import ban

elimination_hints.hint: Make in India encourages private and foreign investment in manufacturing. Nationalisation is the opposite approach.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing investment promotion with nationalisation

elimination_hints.hint: The initiative creates jobs through manufacturing, but its goal is economic growth and industrial development, not population reduction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing economic goals with demographic goals

question_text: GST (Goods and Services Tax) is an example of a change in which dimension of business environment?
explanation: GST is primarily a Legal dimension change because it is a law (Constitutional Amendment and Act of Parliament). It replaced multiple indirect taxes (excise, VAT, service tax) with one unified tax. While GST has economic implications (simpler tax structure, unified market), its nature as a law makes it a legal environment change.
topic: Impact of Government Policy Changes on Business
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Social dimension only
Legal dimension
Technological dimension only
Political dimension only

elimination_hints.hint: Social dimension includes cultural values and consumer attitudes. GST is a tax law, not a social trend.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing legal changes with social changes

elimination_hints.hint: While GST requires a technology platform (GSTN), GST itself is a tax law. Technology is the enabler, not the nature of the change.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the technology used for implementation with the nature of the change

elimination_hints.hint: While the political decision to implement GST was political, GST itself is a law. Laws once enacted become part of the legal environment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the political process with the legal outcome

question_text: Before 1991, the Indian economy was characterised by excessive government controls and licensing, commonly called the __________.
explanation: The pre-1991 Indian economic system was called 'Licence Raj' (also known as 'Permit Raj'). Businesses needed government licences and permits for almost everything — setting up a factory, expanding production, importing goods, even deciding what products to produce. This system led to bureaucratic delays, corruption, and inefficiency, prompting the 1991 reforms.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Free Market
Mixed Economy
Licence Raj
Digital India

elimination_hints.hint: Free market means minimal government intervention. Pre-1991 India had excessive controls — the opposite of a free market.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing controlled economy with free market

elimination_hints.hint: Mixed economy is the official description (mix of public and private sectors). 'Licence Raj' specifically refers to the excessive licensing system within this mixed economy.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the economic system type with the licensing system

elimination_hints.hint: Digital India is a 2015 initiative for digital infrastructure. Licence Raj refers to the pre-1991 system of government permits and controls.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing modern initiatives with historical economic systems

question_text:
Match the following LPG reforms with their correct descriptions:
(i) Liberalisation → (a) Selling government equity in public enterprises
(ii) Privatisation → (b) Integrating with the world economy
(iii) Globalisation → (c) Removing restrictions and controls on business
(iv) Disinvestment → (d) Transferring government enterprises to private ownership
explanation: Correct matching: (i) Liberalisation = Removing restrictions and controls on business (c); (ii) Privatisation = Transferring government enterprises to private ownership (d); (iii) Globalisation = Integrating with the world economy (b); (iv) Disinvestment = Selling government equity in public enterprises (a). Note that disinvestment is a TOOL of privatisation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: This matches Liberalisation with selling equity — but selling equity is Disinvestment. Liberalisation means removing restrictions (c).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing liberalisation with disinvestment

elimination_hints.hint: This matches Liberalisation with world integration — but world integration is Globalisation. Liberalisation removes domestic controls.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing liberalisation with globalisation

elimination_hints.hint: This matches Liberalisation with ownership transfer — but ownership transfer is Privatisation. Every term has a specific meaning.
elimination_hints.misconception: Randomly swapping all definitions
